summary: Gruber responds to a Mic article criticizing Apple's lack of women onstage at the iPhone 7 event. He argues the real problem isn't speaker selection but the underrepresentation of women in Apple's leadership — the people who present are the people who run those product areas. He then turns his criticism on the journalist, Melanie Ehrenkranz, for publishing an off-the-record email from Apple PR and crafting a misleading headline that misrepresented Apple's response. He contends the headline 'We Had a Canadian' was not an actual quote and constituted clickbait that implied callousness Apple didn't express.
key_points: Apple's onstage gender disparity reflects its actual leadership composition — executives present their own products, so the fix is more women in leadership, not token speaking slots
Publishing the Apple PR rep's off-the-record email was technically permissible but violated the courtesy and trust the convention is based on
Mic's headline fabricated a quote by extracting 'We Had a Canadian' from a longer sentence that actually led with 'a gay man' — a grossly misleading truncation
Gruber argues Apple's email was imperfect but reasonable, and that publishing it made Ehrenkranz look worse than Apple
The piece notes that Tim Cook being openly gay counts as meaningful diversity representation that the original article dismissed
money_quotes: The problem is not that women are underrepresented as on stage speakers. The problem is that they are underrepresented in leadership positions.
If Apple simply put more women and people of color on stage, it would be a token gesture. If Apple puts more women and people of color on this page, more of them will appear on stage as a result.
Publishing this email may well be technically within the letter of the accepted rules of journalism. But it strikes me as a dick move.
No reasonable person could say that Mic's headline is an accurate categorization of Apple's response.

Even if you disagree with Apple's response — it should have been handled much better than it was — it's clickbait journalism at its worst to imply that Apple's response to criticism that there weren't enough women on stage was, quote, 'We had a Canadian'.
It's not merely grossly inaccurate, it's implying utter callousness regarding a serious issue.
The first thing Apple's rep actually said after 'We had a' was 'gay man'.
key_insight: Apple's onstage diversity problem is real but rooted in leadership composition, and dishonest journalism that fabricates quotes and sensationalizes responses only undermines the legitimate criticism.
